verbatim_text: He would only get failure, failure, failure all the way for nine years. That's not very informative. So he has to use two things. First, his own judgment of whether this start of a proof is promising. In the LLM analogy, this is like using LLM to give partial feedback for itself. The feedback could take him to the form of word critiques or just a scalar reward for segments of the proof. Now, Andrew Wiles, he would also probably need to solve easier versions of the actual problem. Let's strip the problem down, strip out some of the difficulties, and see what isolate some of the difficult parts of the problem. This would give Wiles a curriculum. So another interesting direction is how to come up with a curriculum. Namely, generate data. One of the distinctions between test and train time in classical machine learning is if you're at train time, you don't keep updating the model, or if you're at test time, you don't keep updating the model. Therefore, you can have lots of parallel use of the model without having different things clobbering. I wonder if you can comment about not single user, but multiple user version of test time training.
